justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Context coupling explicitly identified — Bird calls out 'cross-bounded-context coordination problem' and details how two previously independent contexts become coupled. (2) Data ownership clearly raised — BR-2 states Warehouse is 'sole authority on physical inventory' and the escalation analyzes who owns 'available stock'. (3) TOCTOU/race condition thoroughly identified — edge case #1 names the simultaneous-order problem, BR-3 explains check-then-act is not a guarantee, and the missing-context escalation calls it 'inherently racy'. (4) Stock reservation vs. check distinction surfaced — 'Stock Reservation' is listed as a MISSING concept in ubiquitous language, and a dedicated escalation recommends it as 'the domain-correct solution'. (5) Escalation to MJ for architectural coupling pattern — four structured escalations cover integration pattern, reservation mechanism, and coupling semantics. (6) New cross-context invariant stated — BR-1 and BR-2 define the invariants, and the rejection reasons explain why acceptance criteria cannot be written until ambiguities are resolved.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough TOCTOU analysis — not just flagged but traced through multiple scenarios (concurrent orders, warehouse adjustment between check and pick, indicator staleness)
Correctly identifies 'Stock Reservation' as a MISSING domain concept and explains why its absence makes the check-then-act pattern a race condition
Data ownership is crisp: BR-2 as an invariant, with testable assertion that no write path to StockLevel exists outside Warehouse
Refuses to write acceptance criteria, explaining that doing so would 'encode assumptions as requirements' — exactly avoids the failure mode of silently resolving undefined questions
Surfaces the contradiction between 'real-time' indicator and bounded context independence, offering a hybrid recommendation
Identifies operational blast radius expansion: Warehouse outages now impact Sales, changing the SLA landscape

Edge cases cover not just the happy path race but also partial fulfillment ambiguity, Warehouse unavailability, and blocked-order timeout — all correctly flagged as business decisions, not engineering choices

trials.justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Context coupling explicitly identified — Bird calls out that this 'fundamentally changes Context B's contract' and 'redefines Context B's bounded context boundary and autonomy model.' (2) Data ownership clearly addressed — StockLevel owned by Context A, stock indicator is a read-model projection, BR-5 prohibits cross-context writes. (3) TOCTOU/race condition thoroughly identified — AC-3 tests simultaneous orders for last unit, edge case explicitly covers it with optimistic locking/serialized reservation, BR-3 establishes the reservation-aware invariant. (4) Stock reservation vs. check distinction surfaced — 'Stock Reservation' defined in ubiquitous language as 'Does NOT currently exist in either context — would need to be introduced,' BR-3/BR-4 distinguish authoritative reservation state from eventually-consistent indicator. (5) Escalation to MJ present — first escalation explicitly flags the architectural shift with three options and recommends escalation to product owner; the contradiction type maps to architectural coupling decisions. (6) New cross-context invariant stated — BR-2 and BR-3 define the cross-context invariants.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough context coupling analysis — Bird doesn't just note the coupling, but frames it as a fundamental contract change to Context B's autonomy model with three architectural options (sync, async, hybrid).

TOCTOU problem addressed at multiple layers: invariant (BR-3), acceptance criterion (AC-3), edge case (race condition scenario), and rejection reason — showing deep understanding rather than surface mention.
Stock reservation explicitly called out as non-existent in either context, requiring introduction — exactly the missing mechanism the rubric expects Bird to surface.
BR-4 correctly distinguishes the eventually-consistent read model (indicator) from the strongly-consistent write path (blocking decision) — a subtle but critical domain distinction.
Edge cases go well beyond the rubric requirements: reservation TTL/zombie reservations, partial fulfillment ambiguity, product-not-in-warehouse, and warehouse unavailability are all identified.
Confidence level of 45 is well-calibrated given the number of unresolved ambiguities and the fundamental contradiction in the spec.
Escalations reference 'product owner' generically rather than explicitly naming MJ as the architectural escalation target. The intent is correct but the routing is imprecise for the team structure.

trials.justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Context coupling explicitly identified — 'fundamentally changes the relationship between two previously autonomous bounded contexts' and rejection reason for direct coupling. (2) Data ownership clearly raised — BR-2 states 'Stock Reservation is owned by Warehouse Management, not Sales', Available Stock defined as a Warehouse-derived concept, BR-6 enforces ubiquitous language boundaries preventing StockLevel leaking into Sales. (3) TOCTOU/race condition identified — dedicated edge case for 'two simultaneous orders for the last unit of stock' with serialization requirements, plus AC-6 explicitly tests this. (4) Stock reservation vs. check distinction thoroughly surfaced — Stock Reservation introduced as 'A NEW concept that must be introduced' in ubiquitous language, with extensive analysis of reservation lifecycle including TTL, expiry, and cancellation release. (5) Escalation to MJ present — two formal escalations covering sync-vs-async integration pattern and the meaning of 'automatically block'. (6) New cross-context invariant stated — BR-3 defines atomic reservation requirement across OrderLines as an invariant.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough domain decomposition — introduced Stock Reservation, Available Stock, Stock Availability Projection, and ProductAvailability as distinct domain concepts with clear ownership boundaries
TOCTOU addressed at multiple levels: edge case description, acceptance criterion AC-6, and BR-1's invariant with serialization requirement
BR-2 and BR-6 together create a strong data ownership boundary — not only does Warehouse own writes, but Sales is forbidden from even using Warehouse terminology
Rejection reasons are well-crafted, explicitly blocking the three most common implementation mistakes (direct reads, partial reservations, synchronous coupling)
Edge cases go beyond the rubric requirements — covers reservation expiry during payment, network partition, phantom holds from cancelled orders, and bulk order policy questions
Escalations are structured with options and recommendations rather than just flagging ambiguity, giving MJ actionable choices
Confidence level of 72 is well-calibrated — high enough to reflect strong domain understanding, low enough to acknowledge the significant unspecified integration patterns

Escalations are typed as 'ambiguity' rather than explicitly directed to MJ by name — the rubric expects escalation 'to MJ' specifically, though the architectural nature of the escalations clearly targets MJ's role
Minor: AC-6 prescribes serialization mechanisms (optimistic locking, pessimistic lock, event-sourced) which edges into MJ's architectural territory rather than staying purely in domain rule space
